---
title: "Getting Started with rsparrow"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Getting Started with rsparrow}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>", message = FALSE)
```

## Overview

**rsparrow** is an R package for executing and evaluating SPARROW (SPAtially Referenced
Regressions On Watershed attributes) models. SPARROW models three watershed processes:

1. **Source generation** â€” contaminant inputs from land uses (e.g., agricultural N,
   atmospheric deposition)
2. **Land-to-water delivery** â€” transport to streams via surficial and subsurface pathways,
   modulated by climate and landscape variables
3. **In-stream transport and decay** â€” removal and attenuation as water moves downstream
   through reaches and reservoirs

The package implements static (long-term mean annual) SPARROW models. It generates
reach-level load and yield predictions, source attribution, parameter uncertainty via
bootstrap, and diagnostic plots.

## Installation

```{r install, eval=FALSE}
install.packages("rsparrow")

# Or the development version from GitHub:
# remotes::install_github("Kyle-Hurley/rsparrow")
```

## Step 1: Load the Example Data

rsparrow includes a synthetic 60-reach example dataset, `sparrow_example`, that
demonstrates the full modeling workflow without requiring real data.

```{r load-data}
library(rsparrow)

str(sparrow_example, max.level = 1)
```

The dataset contains:

- **`reaches`** â€” 60-reach dendritic network. Columns include topology (`waterid`,
  `fnode`, `tnode`), drainage area (`demiarea`, `demtarea`), a synthetic
  agricultural-N source (`agN`), a precipitation delivery variable (`ppt`),
  observed loads (`depvar`), and calibration flags (`calsites`).
- **`parameters`** â€” parameter configuration (SOURCE and DELIVF types).
- **`design_matrix`** â€” binary matrix associating sources with delivery variables.
- **`data_dictionary`** â€” optional; maps internal SPARROW variable names to data
  column names (not needed when column names already match).

## Step 2: Inspect the Network Topology

Compute the hydrological sequence â€” the reach ordering from upstream to downstream
that SPARROW uses for load accumulation.

```{r hydseq}
network <- rsparrow_hydseq(sparrow_example$reaches)
head(network[order(network$hydseq), c("waterid", "fnode", "tnode", "hydseq")])
```

Reaches with more negative `hydseq` values are further downstream. The outlet reach
has the most negative value.

## Step 3: Fit the Model

### Composable Workflow

The recommended approach calls two functions separately, letting you inspect the
prepared data object and swap estimation options without re-validating inputs:

```{r composable}
# Step 1: Validate and prepare inputs
sparrow_data <- rsparrow_prepare(
  sparrow_example$reaches,
  sparrow_example$parameters,
  sparrow_example$design_matrix
)
print(sparrow_data)

# Step 2: Estimate the model
model <- rsparrow_estimate(sparrow_data)
```

### Quick-Start (one-call interface)

For a single call that combines preparation and estimation:

```{r fit-model, eval=FALSE}
model <- rsparrow_model(
  sparrow_example$reaches,
  sparrow_example$parameters,
  sparrow_example$design_matrix
)
```

## Step 4: Examine Results

The returned `rsparrow` object supports standard S3 methods:

```{r examine}
# Model summary (fit statistics + parameter estimates)
summary(model)

# Parameter estimates
coef(model)

# Log residuals at calibration sites
residuals(model)

# Parameter covariance matrix
vcov(model)
```

Reach-level load predictions are available immediately (computed when
`if_predict = TRUE`, the default):

```{r predictions}
pred_df <- as.data.frame(model$predictions$predmatrix)
names(pred_df) <- model$predictions$oparmlist
head(pred_df[, c("waterid", "pload_total", "pload_agN", "mpload_total")])
```

## Step 5: Diagnostic Plots

Diagnostic plots use base R graphics:

```{r plots-A, fig.width=7, fig.height=6}
plot(model, type = "residuals")           # observed vs predicted, residuals
```

```{r plots-B, fig.width=7, fig.height=6}
plot(model, type = "residuals", panel = "B")  # Q-Q, boxplots
```

```{r plots-sensitivity, fig.width=7, fig.height=6}
plot(model, type = "sensitivity")         # parameter sensitivity
```

```{r plots-simulation, fig.width=7, fig.height=6}
plot(model, type = "simulation")          # simulation vs calibration predictions
```

## Step 6: Bootstrap Uncertainty

Estimate parameter uncertainty via parametric bootstrap. Use `n_boot >= 200` in
practice; here we use 50 for speed:

```{r bootstrap}
model <- rsparrow_bootstrap(model, n_boot = 50, seed = 42)

# Bootstrapped parameter distributions (iterations Ă— parameters)
head(model$bootstrap$bEstimate)
```

```{r plots-bootstrap, fig.width=7, fig.height=6}
plot(model, type = "bootstrap")           # bootstrap parameter distributions
```

## Step 7: Holdout Validation

To use holdout validation, designate at least one monitoring site as a validation
site by setting `calsites = 0`. Here we hold out one site from the five available:

```{r validation}
reaches_v <- sparrow_example$reaches
# Mark the first monitoring site as a validation site
cal_rows <- which(reaches_v$calsites == 1 & reaches_v$depvar > 0)
reaches_v$calsites[cal_rows[1]] <- 0

model_v <- rsparrow_estimate(
  rsparrow_prepare(reaches_v, sparrow_example$parameters, sparrow_example$design_matrix),
  if_validate = TRUE
)

# Compute validation metrics at the held-out site
model_v <- rsparrow_validate(model_v)
model_v$validation$vANOVA.list
```

## Step 8: Scenario Analysis

Evaluate the effect of changing source inputs. Source multipliers less than 1 reduce
the source; greater than 1 increase it:

```{r scenario}
# Simulate a 50% reduction in agricultural N loading
model <- rsparrow_scenario(model, source_changes = list(agN = 0.5))

# Scenario predictions (loads roughly halved)
scen_df <- as.data.frame(model$predictions$scenarios$predmatrix)
names(scen_df) <- model$predictions$scenarios$oparmlist
baseline <- pred_df$pload_total
scenario  <- scen_df$pload_total
head(data.frame(waterid = pred_df$waterid, baseline_load = round(baseline, 1),
                scenario_load = round(scenario, 1),
                pct_change = round((scenario / baseline - 1) * 100, 1)))
```

## API Reference

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `rsparrow_prepare()` | Validate inputs; return `rsparrow_data` object |
| `rsparrow_estimate()` | Fit NLLS model; return `rsparrow` object |
| `rsparrow_model()` | One-call convenience wrapper for the two steps above |
| `predict()` | Reach-level load/yield predictions |
| `rsparrow_bootstrap()` | Parametric bootstrap for parameter uncertainty |
| `rsparrow_validate()` | Holdout validation |
| `rsparrow_scenario()` | Source-change scenario analysis |
| `rsparrow_hydseq()` | Compute hydrological sequence |
| `write_rsparrow_results()` | Write results to disk |

## Further Reading

- `?rsparrow_prepare` â€” data preparation and required column names
- `?rsparrow_estimate` â€” estimation options (hessian, weights, units)
- `?rsparrow_model` â€” convenience wrapper documentation
- `?sparrow_example` â€” description of the example dataset
- SPARROW methodology: Alexander, R.B., et al. (2008). *Differences in phosphorus and
  nitrogen delivery to the Gulf of Mexico from the Mississippi River basin.*
  Environmental Science & Technology, 42(3), 822â€“830.
- USGS SPARROW resources: <https://www.usgs.gov/mission-areas/water-resources/science/sparrow-modeling>

## Citation

Gorman Sanisaca, Lillian, Hurley, Kyle P., and Alexander, Richard B. (2025).
RSPARROW 2.1.0: An R system for SPARROW modeling. U.S. Geological Survey.

## Disclaimer

This software has been approved for release by the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS).
Although the software has been subjected to rigorous review, the USGS reserves the
right to update the software as needed pursuant to further analysis and review. No
warranty, expressed or implied, is made by the USGS or the U.S. Government as to
the functionality of the software and related material nor shall the fact of release
constitute any such warranty.

Any use of trade, firm, or product names is for descriptive purposes only and does
not imply endorsement by the U.S. Government.
